_meta.title: Register Allocation Linear Scan Vs Graph Coloring

questions.question: Two virtual registers 'interfere' in register allocation when:
options: They are defined in the same basic block
They are both live at the same program point
They are used by the same instruction
They have the same type
questions.explanation: Two variables interfere if they are both live at the same time — meaning both hold values that will be used later. They cannot share the same physical register.

questions.question: In the Chaitin-Briggs graph coloring algorithm, a node is 'simplify-safe' (guaranteed to be colorable) when it has:
options: Zero neighbors
Fewer than K neighbors (where K = number of registers)
An even number of neighbors
At least one spill candidate as a neighbor
questions.explanation: A node with fewer than K neighbors can always be assigned a color — there are at most K-1 colors used by neighbors, so at least one of the K colors is free.

questions.question: Why do JIT compilers (like HotSpot and V8) prefer linear scan over graph coloring for register allocation?
options: Linear scan produces better code quality
Linear scan handles more register architectures
Linear scan is significantly faster (O(V log V) vs O(V²))
Linear scan does not require liveness analysis
questions.explanation: Linear scan runs in O(V log V) time compared to O(V²) for graph coloring. In JIT compilation, compilation speed matters because it happens at runtime. The slightly worse code quality is an acceptable trade-off.

questions.question: During the 'simplify' phase of Chaitin-Briggs, what happens if every remaining node has degree ≥ K?
options: The algorithm terminates with an error
A spill candidate is selected and removed from the graph
Additional registers are allocated from the stack
Nodes are merged through coalescing
questions.explanation: When no node has fewer than K neighbors, the algorithm selects one node as a spill candidate (typically the one with the highest degree or highest spill cost), marks it, and removes it to continue simplifying.

questions.question: Why is register allocation an important compiler optimization?
options: It reduces the number of instructions in the program
It maps virtual registers to physical registers, minimizing costly spills to memory
It eliminates all memory accesses
It ensures every variable has a unique register
questions.explanation: CPUs have a limited number of physical registers (16 on x86-64, 32 on RISC-V). Register allocation assigns virtual registers to physical ones, spilling excess values to the stack. Good allocation reduces memory traffic; spilling is often the single biggest performance bottleneck.
